source.title: Directed evolution of phosphite dehydrogenase to cycle noncanonical redox cofactors via universal growth selection platform

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper addresses the need for enzymes that can recycle noncanonical redox cofactors like NMN+ for orthogonal biocatalysis. The main challenge is the lack of high-throughput selection methods for NMN+-dependent enzymes. The authors hypothesize that a growth-coupled selection platform can be built by engineering a glutathione reductase (Gor) to specifically use NMNH, linking NMN+ reduction to cell survival under oxidative stress. This platform is then used to evolve phosphite dehydrogenase (PTDH) for NMN+ utilization.

section_gists.heading: Design of the growth-based selection platform for NMN+-reducing enzymes
section_gists.gist: The selection platform is based on the E. coli SHuffle strain (Δgor ΔtrxB), which has an oxidative intracellular environment and requires functional Gor to survive oxidative stress from diamide. By engineering Gor to specifically use NMNH instead of NAD(P)H, any NMN+-reducing enzyme can be made essential for cell growth by supplying NMNH to Gor for glutathione reduction.
process_signals: Hypothesis: SHuffle strain cannot combat oxidative stress effectively and requires functional Gor to survive diamide exposure.
If Gor is engineered to use NMNH, then NMN+-reducing enzymes become life-essential.
methods: Use of E. coli SHuffle strain with knockouts of gor and trxB.

section_gists.heading: Engineering an NMNH-specific Gor
section_gists.gist: Rational design on E. coli Gor targeted residues G174, G176, I178 near the NMNH phosphate. I178T showed 4-fold enhanced NMNH activity. G174H also improved. To increase orthogonality, previously identified R198M-R204L mutations (which abolish NADPH activity) were combined with I178T to create Gor Ortho (I178T-R198M-R204L). Gor Ortho has ~60,000-fold cofactor specificity switch from NADPH to NMNH based on catalytic efficiency, with drastically reduced NAD(P)H activity. Kinetic characterization showed Gor Ortho cannot be saturated with NAD(P)H at physiological concentrations, supporting its specificity in vivo.
process_signals: Hypothesis: ribose and phosphate of NMN(H) can accept new polar interactions to replace missing AMP interactions.
First round rational design: 8 single mutants tested; I178T and G174H improved NMNH activity.
Combining with R198M-R204L to reduce NADPH activity: modest decrease in NMNH activity but huge drop in NADPH activity.
Gor Ortho selected for further testing.
methods: Rational design targeting G174, G176, I178.
Kinetic assays for NMNH, NADPH, NADH.
In vitro characterization of catalytic efficiency.
metrics: Fold changes in activity and specificity.
failures_or_limits: Gor Ortho still has relatively low catalytic efficiency for NMNH, but intracellular NMN(H) concentration can be modulated by exogenous supplementation.

section_gists.heading: Validation of the growth-based selection platform
section_gists.gist: The platform was validated using an engineered GDH (Bs GDH Triple) that reduces NMN+. When co-expressed with Gor Ortho in SHuffle, cell growth increased with NMN+ supplementation. Wild-type Bs GDH (NAD(P)+-specific) did not support growth. SHuffle with wild-type Ec Gor grew independently of NMN+ due to NADPH use. This confirms the platform specifically reports NMN+-reducing activity.
process_signals: Positive control: engineered GDH supports growth in NMN+-dependent manner.
Negative control: wild-type GDH does not support growth.
Specificity: wild-type Gor rescues growth without NMN+.
methods: Co-expression of Gor Ortho and GDH variants in SHuffle.
Growth assays on M9 agar with diamide, glucose, NMN+.
metrics: Cell growth (colony formation) as function of NMN+ concentration.

section_gists.heading: Enable phosphite dehydrogenase to use NMN+ by rational design
section_gists.gist: Starting from thermostable PTDH (TS-PTDH), a two-stage approach: rational design on first-shell residues, then site-saturated mutagenesis with growth selection. Rational design targeted 6 residues to form polar contacts with NMN+. 14 variants tested. A155N showed ~3-fold increase in specific activity; T101K-A155G ~2-fold. A155N had ~3-fold higher catalytic efficiency for NMN+. Modeling suggests A155N forms H-bond with K76, pre-organizing phosphate binding; T101K-A155G creates salt bridge with phosphate after A155G clears space.
process_signals: Two-stage workflow: rational design to seed exploration, then high-throughput screening.
Rational design hypothesis: introduce polar contacts to NMN+ phosphate/ribose.
Sequence entropy analysis showed high conservation, but structure-guided design used instead.
A155N and T101K-A155G identified as improved variants.
A155N chosen as template for next round.
failures_or_limits: Only modest improvements (2-3 fold) from rational design.
Many designed variants showed no improvement.
Need for further evolution to achieve higher activity.

section_gists.heading: Evolving phosphite dehydrogenase using high-throughput growth selection
section_gists.gist: Site-saturated mutagenesis library on TS-PTDH A155N targeting E175, A176, L208 (adenosine binding cleft). Library (~2.5e6 transformants) transformed into SHuffle with Gor Ortho. First selection round (M9 agar, 0.5 mM diamide, 5 g/L yeast extract, 5 mM NMN+) yielded 8 colonies; 4 variants with improved activity. LY-6 (A155N-E175Q-A176S) and LY-7 (A155N-E175W-A176G-L208V) showed 17-fold and 11-fold increased catalytic efficiency for NMN+ vs A155N. Second round with increased stringency (1 g/L yeast extract) yielded LY-13 (A155N-E175A-A176F) with 110-fold improvement over TS-PTDH and 44-fold over A155N. Variants also showed improved NADP+ activity with minimal loss of NAD+ activity.
process_signals: Library targeted three positions simultaneously with NNK codons.
First round: moderate selection pressure; isolated LY-6 and LY-7.
Second round: increased selection pressure by lowering yeast extract; isolated LY-13 with higher activity.
Convergent evolution: different mutations achieve similar mechanism.
Trade-off: improved NMN+ and NADP+ activity, slight decrease in NAD+ activity for some variants.
methods: Site-saturated mutagenesis (NNK) at E175, A176, L208.
Transformation into SHuffle with Gor Ortho.
Growth selection on M9 agar plates with diamide, phosphite, NMN+.
Two rounds of selection with varying yeast extract concentrations.
metrics: Catalytic efficiency (kcat/KM) for NMN+, NAD+, NADP+.
failures_or_limits: Only 8 colonies picked from first round; may have missed other variants.
LY-13 still has relatively low absolute catalytic efficiency (0.44 mM-1 s-1).

section_gists.heading: Elucidating the mechanism of enhanced NMN+-dependent activity in engineered PTDHs
section_gists.gist: Rosetta modeling revealed that evolved variants recapitulate the role of the missing AMP moiety through additional residue packing in the adenine cleft. LY-6: E175Q forms H-bonds with M153 backbone and A176S hydroxyl, stabilizing loops. LY-7: A176G allows E175W to pack against L208V, filling the adenine pocket with hydrophobic interactions. LY-13: E175A shrinks, A176F packs against L208 and P209, filling void. Common theme: mutations emulate AMP binding to stabilize closed conformation.
process_signals: Hypothesis: mutations compensate for missing AMP by filling the adenine binding pocket.
LY-6 uses H-bond network; LY-7 and LY-13 use hydrophobic packing.
Convergent evolution towards same mechanism.
methods: Structural analysis of mutations in the binding pocket.
failures_or_limits: Mechanistic insights are based on computational modeling, not experimental structures.

section_gists.heading: Application of the engineered PTDHs in biocatalysis using noncanonical cofactors
section_gists.gist: Engineered PTDHs were tested in vitro for NMN+ cycling with enoate reductase XenA for ketoisophorone reduction. LY-13 achieved >99% conversion with 0.5 mM NMN+, while TS-PTDH required 5 mM. LY-13 also showed high total turnover number (TTN) and maintained activity at low NMN+ concentrations. Additionally, LY-13 could recycle the simpler cofactor biomimetic BNA+ (1-benzyl nicotinamide), enabling levodione production with BNA+ cycling. This demonstrates broad utility of engineered PTDHs for noncanonical cofactor regeneration.
process_signals: LY-13 outperforms TS-PTDH at low NMN+ concentrations.
LY-13 can use BNA+, expanding scope.
Practical application demonstrated with high conversion and TTN.
methods: In vitro biocatalysis with PTDH variants and XenA.
Cofactor cycling with phosphite as sacrificial reductant.
failures_or_limits: BNA+ cycling slower than NMN+; may require further optimization.
Only one model reaction tested; broader substrate scope not explored.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Three main contributions: (1) Gor-based growth selection platform for NMN+-utilizing enzymes; (2) highly proficient PTDH variants for noncanonical cofactor recycling with high TTN and low KM; (3) general design principle of recapitulating AMP binding to enhance activity. Growth selection enabled observation of convergent evolution. Platform can be adapted to other cofactors by engineering Gor. AMP-recapitulating mutations may induce closed conformation, lowering KM and increasing kcat. Limitations: need for case-by-case metabolic tailoring; current PTDHs still have room for improvement in catalytic efficiency. Future work: structural studies, MD simulations, adaptive laboratory evolution.
process_signals: Growth selection throughput enabled discovery of convergent evolution.
Design principle: emulate native cofactor binding.
Platform is universal for NMNH-dependent enzymes.
Bottleneck: tailoring metabolism for each new enzyme.
Future: engineer Gor for other cofactors, use continuous evolution.
failures_or_limits: Growth selection requires case-by-case metabolic engineering.
Engineered PTDHs still have suboptimal catalytic parameters.
Mechanism of AMP recapitulation needs experimental validation.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research progressed from building a universal growth selection platform (Gor engineering) to applying it for directed evolution of PTDH.
Rational design provided initial activity improvements but was insufficient; high-throughput selection was necessary to achieve significant gains.
Convergent evolution observed in PTDH variants suggests a common design principle of filling the AMP binding pocket.
The platform's success with two different enzymes (GDH and PTDH) indicates broad applicability.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Whether the engineered Gor Ortho can be further improved for higher NMNH activity.
If the growth selection platform can be adapted to other noncanonical cofactors like BNA+ by engineering Gor.
The exact structural basis of improved activity (needs crystallography or MD simulations).
Whether the evolved PTDHs can be further optimized for industrial applications (e.g., higher kcat, broader substrate scope).
The extent to which the AMP-recapitulating design principle applies to other enzymes.

events.summary: The SHuffle strain cannot combat oxidative stress effectively and requires a functional Gor to survive diamide exposure.
The ribose and phosphate groups of NMN(H) can accept new polar interactions to replace missing AMP interactions.
Rational design targeted residues G174, G176, I178 near the NMNH phosphate to create new interactions.
I178T showed 4-fold enhanced NMNH-dependent activity compared to wild type.
Combined I178T with R198M-R204L to create Gor Ortho, drastically reducing NADPH activity while maintaining NMNH activity.

events.summary: Rosetta modeling suggested that A155N forms a hydrogen bond with K76, reinforcing the salt bridge with the NMN+ phosphate, potentially pre-organizing K76 for cofactor binding.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk covers validation of the selection platform, rational design of PTDH, growth selection experiments, and mechanistic analysis of evolved variants.

events.summary: Structural modeling of PTDH variants reveals how mutations recapitulate AMP binding to enhance NMN+ activity.
Growth-based selection requires case-by-case metabolic tailoring.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers structural analysis of evolved PTDH variants, application in biocatalysis with NMN+ and BNA+, and discussion of platform contributions and limitations.

events.summary: Hypothesis that AMP-recapitulating mutations in engineered PTDHs induce closed conformation, lowering KM and enhancing kcat for NMN+.
Design principle: AMP-recapitulating mutations may be added to toolbox for rationally engineering BNA+-utilizing enzymes.
Fine tuning catalytic parameters for non-canonical cofactors has proven difficult.
Growth selection platform is uniquely suited to select for enzymes with improved catalytic efficiencies.
Construction of PTDH combinatorial site-saturation mutagenesis library with degenerate codons at E175, A176, L208.
Selection of TS-PTDH library using growth rescue in SHuffle strain with Gor Ortho and NMN+.
Determination of total turnover number (TTN) for TS-PTDH LY-13 in NMN+ cycling with XenA.
numbers_or_metrics: TTN calculated by moles levodione produced divided by moles LY-13

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Engineering enzymes to recycle noncanonical redox cofactors like NMN+ for orthogonal biocatalysis, hindered by lack of high-throughput selection methods.

canonical_events.summary: Hypothesis that E. coli SHuffle strain (Δgor ΔtrxB) requires functional Gor to survive oxidative stress from diamide, enabling growth-coupled selection for NMN+-reducing enzymes if Gor is engineered to use NMNH.
Hypothesis that ribose and phosphate groups of NMN(H) can accept new polar interactions to replace missing AMP interactions in Gor.
Rational design targeting Gor residues G174, G176, I178 near NMNH phosphate to create new interactions.
I178T mutant shows 4-fold enhanced NMNH-dependent activity compared to wild-type Gor.
Combining I178T with R198M-R204L to create Gor Ortho, drastically reducing NADPH activity while maintaining NMNH activity.
Validation of growth selection platform using engineered GDH (Bs GDH Triple) co-expressed with Gor Ortho in SHuffle; growth depends on NMN+ supplementation, confirming platform specificity.
A155N variant shows ~3-fold increase in specific activity and ~3-fold higher catalytic efficiency for NMN+ compared to wild-type TS-PTDH; T101K-A155G shows ~2-fold increase.
Rosetta modeling suggests A155N forms hydrogen bond with K76, pre-organizing phosphate binding for NMN+.
Construction of site-saturation mutagenesis library on TS-PTDH A155N targeting E175, A176, L208; library size 4.7×10^7 transformants.

First round growth selection (0.5 mM diamide, 5 g/L yeast extract, 5 mM NMN+) yields 8 colonies; LY-6 (A155N-E175Q-A176S) and LY-7 (A155N-E175W-A176G-L208V) show 17-fold and 11-fold increased catalytic efficiency for NMN+ vs A155N.

Second round growth selection with increased stringency (1 g/L yeast extract) yields LY-13 (A155N-E175A-A176F) with 110-fold improvement over TS-PTDH and 44-fold over A155N in catalytic efficiency for NMN+ (0.44 mM⁻¹ s⁻¹).
Structural modeling reveals evolved PTDH variants recapitulate AMP binding via hydrophobic packing or hydrogen bond networks in the adenine cleft, inducing closed conformation.
Application of LY-13 in NMN+ cycling with XenA for ketoisophorone reduction achieves >99% conversion at 0.5 mM NMN+; LY-13 also recycles BNA+ for levodione production.
Growth-based selection requires case-by-case metabolic tailoring; fine-tuning catalytic parameters for non-canonical cofactors remains difficult.

edges.reason: Hypothesis about SHuffle strain's dependence on Gor motivates engineering Gor for NMNH specificity.
Hypothesis about NMN(H) interactions guides rational design of Gor mutants.
Rational design leads to experimental testing of I178T mutant.
Improved NMNH activity of I178T is combined with NADPH-abolishing mutations to create Gor Ortho.
Gor Ortho enables validation of growth selection platform with engineered GDH.
Rosetta modeling provides mechanistic insight into A155N improvement.
A155N serves as template for site-saturation library construction.
Library is subjected to first round growth selection, yielding LY-6 and LY-7.
Increased selection stringency in second round yields LY-13 with higher activity.
Structural modeling explains how mutations in LY-6, LY-7, LY-13 recapitulate AMP binding.
LY-13's improved catalytic efficiency enables practical biocatalysis with NMN+ and BNA+.
Application reveals limitations of growth selection and difficulty in fine-tuning parameters.

must_cover_points: Hypothesis of growth-coupled selection using engineered Gor
Rational design and engineering of Gor Ortho
Validation of selection platform with GDH
Rational design of PTDH yielding A155N
Site-saturation library construction and growth selection
Isolation and kinetic characterization of LY-6, LY-7, LY-13
Biocatalytic application with NMN+ and BNA+
